sections.plain_summary: Non-coeliac gluten sensitivity (NCGS) is a condition where eating gluten triggers digestive and other symptoms, but blood tests for coeliac disease are negative and there is no intestinal damage. The exact cause is not fully understood, but symptoms usually improve when gluten is removed from the diet.
sections.detailed_summary: Non-coeliac gluten sensitivity (NCGS) is a poorly defined condition characterised by gastrointestinal and extra-intestinal symptoms triggered by gluten ingestion in people who do not have coeliac disease (negative tissue transglutaminase antibodies, normal intestinal biopsy) or wheat allergy (negative IgE testing). The underlying mechanism remains unclear—proposed mechanisms include activation of innate immunity, sensitivity to other wheat components (like fermentable carbohydrates or amylase-trypsin inhibitors), or psychological factors. NCGS is diagnosed largely by symptom response to gluten exclusion, as no specific biomarker exists. Prevalence estimates vary widely because diagnostic criteria are loose. Most people with NCGS report improvement within days to weeks of eliminating gluten, though strict adherence is needed to assess true response.

prevalence.summary: Estimated to affect somewhere between 1 and 6% of the population, though exact figures are contentious because there is no agreed diagnostic test.
prevalence.estimate: 1–6% of the general population; some studies suggest up to 10% in certain populations
prevalence.notes: Wide variation reflects the lack of a gold-standard biomarker and reliance on self-reported symptoms. Many people self-diagnose without clinical input. Rates may be higher in people already seeking care for gastrointestinal complaints. Overlap with irritable bowel syndrome (IBS) and other functional disorders makes true prevalence difficult to pin down.

subgroups.name: Predominantly gastrointestinal NCGS
subgroups.description: Characterised primarily by digestive symptoms such as bloating, diarrhoea, constipation, and abdominal pain, with few or no systemic symptoms.
distinguishing_features: Symptoms resolve quickly (within days) after gluten elimination
Symptoms flare predictably after gluten consumption
No evidence of wheat allergy or coeliac disease
May overlap significantly with IBS or functional dyspepsia

subgroups.name: Predominantly extra-intestinal NCGS
subgroups.description: Characterised by neurological, joint, skin, or systemic symptoms triggered or worsened by gluten, with minimal or no digestive complaints.
distinguishing_features: Headache, 'brain fog', fatigue, or joint pain as primary symptoms
Less obvious link between gluten intake and symptom onset
More difficult to confirm because symptoms are subjective and slow-onset

subgroups.name: Atypical or mixed-phenotype NCGS
subgroups.description: A spectrum presentation with variable gastrointestinal and extra-intestinal features, sometimes overlapping with other functional or autoimmune conditions.
distinguishing_features: Delayed symptom onset (hours to days after gluten intake)
Frequent comorbidity with IBS, fibromyalgia, or chronic fatigue syndrome
Response to gluten elimination may be partial or variable

symptoms.notes: Often occurs within 1–2 hours of gluten intake; may worsen throughout the day. One of the most commonly reported symptoms.
Can range from mild cramping to severe pain; timing and character vary between individuals.
Typically occurs within hours of gluten ingestion; may be watery or frequent.
Less common than diarrhoea but reported by some; may alternate with looser stools.
May occur shortly after eating gluten-containing foods.
Often reported after gluten ingestion; severity varies; can persist for days.
May occur within hours of gluten intake; some report triggered or worsened migraines.

symptoms.symptom: Brain fog, difficulty concentrating, or memory problems
symptoms.notes: Subjective and difficult to measure; more commonly reported in extra-intestinal phenotype.
Non-specific symptoms; may involve knees, hips, or small joints; relationship to gluten less clear than with digestive symptoms.
Less common; onset may be delayed; difficult to distinguish from dermatitis herpetiformis or food allergy.
Anecdotally reported; causality unclear and hard to establish without controlled study.
Uncommon; may improve after gluten elimination, though not specific to NCGS.

key_patterns: Symptoms appear or worsen within minutes to a few hours of eating gluten-containing foods and improve within days to a week of avoidance
Symptoms are reproducible—they occur consistently when gluten is reintroduced and resolve when it is eliminated
A mix of gastrointestinal symptoms (bloating, pain, diarrhoea) and/or systemic symptoms (fatigue, headache, joint pain) in the absence of anaemia, weight loss, or alarm features
No previous diagnosis of coeliac disease, wheat allergy, or other clear medical explanation for symptoms
Symptoms triggered by wheat, barley, or rye specifically (not just general food sensitivities)

at_home.name: Gluten elimination trial (elimination-rechallenge)
at_home.description: Stop all gluten-containing foods (wheat, barley, rye) for 2–4 weeks and carefully document symptom changes. If symptoms improve significantly, reintroduce gluten in a deliberate meal and watch for symptom recurrence over the next 1–2 days. Symptom return on rechallenge suggests NCGS.
at_home.safety_notes: This trial is safe but vulnerable to placebo effect and expectation bias. Ensure you truly eliminate gluten (hidden sources in sauces, processed foods, and medications can undermine the trial). If symptoms do not improve after strict elimination, NCGS is less likely. Do not use this trial to diagnose conditions requiring urgent investigation (e.g. persistent weight loss, bloody stools, anaemia).

at_home.name: Food and symptom diary
at_home.description: Record all foods and drinks consumed and any symptoms (time, severity, type) for at least 2–4 weeks. Look for patterns linking gluten-containing meals to symptom onset. Note timing, severity, and whether symptoms were triggered by other factors (stress, sleep, menstrual cycle).
at_home.safety_notes: Diaries are subjective and prone to recall bias and confirmation bias. Multiple food triggers and non-dietary factors (stress, infection, hormones) often confound the picture. Use the diary as a starting point, not as definitive proof.

clinical.name: Coeliac disease serology (tissue transglutaminase IgA antibodies, tTG-IgA)
clinical.description: Blood test measuring autoantibodies against transglutaminase. A negative result (in the setting of ongoing gluten intake) rules out coeliac disease. Must be done while eating gluten; a false-negative can occur if gluten has already been eliminated.
clinical.availability: Ordered by primary care doctors or gastroenterologists; results available within 1–2 weeks. May be covered by insurance as part of evaluation for suspected coeliac disease.

clinical.name: Endomysial antibodies (EMA)
clinical.description: Confirmatory blood test for coeliac disease using indirect immunofluorescence. Used to confirm a positive tTG-IgA. A negative result further supports non-coeliac diagnosis.
clinical.availability: Usually ordered by gastroenterologists if tTG-IgA is positive; may be done on the same initial blood draw.

clinical.name: Upper gastrointestinal endoscopy and duodenal biopsy
clinical.description: Direct visualisation of the small intestine via endoscope with tissue sampling. The gold standard for coeliac disease diagnosis. A normal biopsy (no villous atrophy or increased intraepithelial lymphocytes) rules out coeliac disease and supports NCGS if gluten-triggered symptoms are present.
clinical.availability: Ordered by gastroenterologists; procedure performed in an outpatient endoscopy suite under sedation. Requires referral and a wait; associated with small procedural risks.

clinical.name: Wheat allergy testing (skin prick test and/or specific IgE)
clinical.description: Allergy-focused tests to rule out IgE-mediated wheat allergy, which presents with acute symptoms (hives, throat swelling, anaphylaxis) rather than delayed gastrointestinal upset. Negative results help distinguish NCGS from allergy.
clinical.availability: Ordered by allergists or primary care doctors. Skin prick testing done in-office; specific IgE blood testing results available within days to 1–2 weeks.

clinical.name: HLA-DQ2 or HLA-DQ5 genetic testing
clinical.description: Genetic markers associated with coeliac disease susceptibility. Useful as a negative predictor: if both HLA-DQ2 and HLA-DQ5 are absent, coeliac disease is very unlikely. Some researchers suggest HLA testing may refine NCGS phenotyping, but clinical utility remains experimental.
clinical.availability: Specialist centres or research settings; not routine in primary care. If ordered, results available in 1–2 weeks.

clinical.name: FODMAP breath testing
clinical.description: Hydrogen and methane breath test to assess malabsorption of fermentable carbohydrates (FODMAP). Elevated breath levels suggest poor absorption, which can contribute to bloating and gas. Some researchers hypothesise that FODMAP content in wheat (not gluten itself) drives symptoms in some cases.
clinical.availability: Specialist gastroenterology centres; not routine. Requires scheduling and dietary preparation (low-FODMAP diet for 24 hours prior).

related_conditions.name: Coeliac disease
related_conditions.explanation: Both are triggered by gluten, but coeliac disease involves autoimmunity and small-intestinal damage (villous atrophy), whereas NCGS shows no such changes. Serology and biopsy distinguish them. Coeliac disease is associated with more systemic complications (anaemia, osteoporosis, malabsorption) and requires stricter gluten avoidance.

related_conditions.name: Wheat allergy (IgE-mediated)
related_conditions.explanation: Wheat allergy is an immediate hypersensitivity reaction (minutes to 2 hours) with hives, angioedema, or anaphylaxis. NCGS causes delayed gastrointestinal symptoms. Allergy testing (IgE, skin prick) distinguishes them.

related_conditions.name: Irritable bowel syndrome (IBS)
related_conditions.explanation: NCGS and IBS share overlapping symptoms (bloating, abdominal pain, altered bowel habit) and have high comorbidity. Some studies suggest that 30–50% of people with IBS may also experience gluten sensitivity. However, not all IBS is gluten-related; other triggers (stress, FODMAP, infection) are common.

related_conditions.name: FODMAP sensitivity
related_conditions.explanation: Wheat contains fermentable carbohydrates (fructans) that are poorly absorbed and trigger bloating and gas in susceptible individuals. Some researchers propose that NCGS symptoms may be driven by FODMAP content rather than gluten per se. A low-FODMAP diet may help distinguish true gluten sensitivity from FODMAP intolerance.

related_conditions.name: Functional dyspepsia
related_conditions.explanation: Symptoms overlap with NCGS (nausea, postprandial fullness, upper abdominal discomfort). Co-occurrence is common, and gluten elimination may improve symptoms in both conditions.

related_conditions.name: Chronic fatigue syndrome / Myalgic encephalomyelitis (ME/CFS)
related_conditions.explanation: Some individuals with ME/CFS report symptom improvement on a gluten-free diet, though a causal link is not established. The relationship may reflect shared immunological mechanisms or dietary factors.

related_conditions.name: Fibromyalgia
related_conditions.explanation: Joint and muscle pain reported in some NCGS cases overlaps with fibromyalgia. Anecdotal reports of symptom improvement with gluten avoidance exist, but causality is unproven.

related_conditions.name: Dermatitis herpetiformis
related_conditions.explanation: A blistering skin condition associated with coeliac disease and IgA deposition in the skin. Gluten-triggered skin symptoms in NCGS (rashes, eczema) may share some immune mechanisms but are less well-characterised.

strategies.name: Strict gluten elimination diet
strategies.description: Remove all wheat, barley, rye, and foods containing gluten. Read labels carefully; gluten is hidden in many processed foods, sauces, gravies, and medications. Use certified gluten-free products where needed. Most people see symptom improvement within 3–7 days if NCGS is present.
strategies.notes: This is the only proven treatment for NCGS. Effectiveness depends on strict adherence; even trace amounts of gluten can trigger symptoms in sensitive individuals. Placebo effect is strong, so reintroduction testing is helpful to confirm true response. Hidden gluten sources include soy sauce, vinegars, and some spice blends.

strategies.name: Low-FODMAP diet (if concurrent FODMAP sensitivity)
strategies.description: Reduce fermentable carbohydrates (fructans, polyols, lactose) to identify whether FODMAP sensitivity contributes to symptoms. Restrict onions, garlic, wheat, certain fruits, and legumes for 2–4 weeks, then systematically reintroduce to identify triggers.
strategies.notes: Useful if gluten elimination alone is insufficient or if symptoms persist. May be more effective than gluten elimination alone in some cases. Should be supervised by a dietitian to avoid unnecessary restriction and nutritional deficiency. Works best when symptoms improve on a low-FODMAP diet but worsen on high-FODMAP foods.

strategies.name: Probiotics and dietary fibre
strategies.description: Probiotic supplements or fermented foods (yogurt, sauerkraut, kimchi) may support gut barrier function and reduce bloating. Adequate soluble fibre (oats, flaxseed, psyllium) can help stabilise bowel movements, though fibre should be introduced gradually.
strategies.notes: Evidence for probiotics in NCGS is weak and strain-dependent. Individual response varies widely. Fibre can initially worsen bloating if introduced too quickly. Choose low-FODMAP fibre sources where possible (psyllium, oat bran).

strategies.name: Antispasmodic medications (if abdominal cramping)
strategies.description: Dicyclomine, hyoscyamine, or peppermint oil can reduce intestinal spasm and cramping. Used short-term to manage pain while dietary changes take effect.
strategies.notes: Effective for pain relief but does not address the underlying trigger. Not a substitute for gluten elimination. Peppermint oil (enteric-coated) is often tried first due to fewer side effects.

strategies.name: Anti-diarrhoeal agents (if diarrhoea)
strategies.description: Loperamide or bismuth subsalicylate can reduce diarrhoea and urgency. Use sparingly and only after diet-triggered cases are ruled out.
strategies.notes: Avoid prolonged use; masking symptoms may delay diagnosis of other conditions. Not necessary once gluten is eliminated in true NCGS.

strategies.name: Stress management and sleep optimisation
strategies.description: Stress and poor sleep worsen gut sensitivity and symptom perception. Practice relaxation techniques (yoga, meditation, breathing exercises), maintain consistent sleep schedules, and address anxiety. Gut-brain axis dysfunction is common in functional disorders.
strategies.notes: Psychological stress amplifies symptom perception in IBS and NCGS. Cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT) and gut-directed hypnotherapy show benefit in IBS and may help NCGS. Sleep deprivation lowers pain threshold and worsens gut symptoms.

strategies.name: Gradual, balanced reintroduction diet
strategies.description: After symptom resolution on a gluten-free diet, systematically reintroduce other eliminated foods (if any) and gluten to map true triggers. Reintroduce one food at a time over 3–5 days and observe for symptom recurrence.
strategies.notes: Helps distinguish gluten sensitivity from other food triggers or nocebo effect. A true reintroduction trial with blinded gluten/placebo would be more rigorous but is rarely practical outside research settings.

strategies.name: Digestive enzyme supplements (pancreatic enzymes, α-galactosidase)
strategies.description: Digestive enzymes may reduce bloating and gas, especially if pancreatic insufficiency or oligosaccharide malabsorption (e.g. from beans) is contributing. Beano or similar products contain α-galactosidase to reduce gas from legumes.
strategies.notes: Weak evidence in NCGS. More useful for specific triggering foods (beans, cruciferous vegetables) than for gluten itself. Pancreatic enzyme supplements are indicated only if pancreatic insufficiency is confirmed.

strategies.name: Zinc carnosine and L-glutamine (intestinal barrier support)
strategies.description: Amino acids and amino acid derivatives that may support intestinal barrier integrity and reduce permeability. Proposed for functional gut disorders, though evidence is limited.
strategies.notes: Weak evidence in NCGS; individual response varies. May take weeks to show effect. Not a substitute for dietary intervention.

things_to_avoid: Wheat, barley, and rye (and foods containing them) in people with confirmed NCGS—these trigger symptoms and must be strictly avoided once diagnosis is established.
Hidden gluten sources: soy sauce, gravies, salad dressings, spice mixes, cured meats, and some medications. Read labels meticulously.
Cross-contamination: use dedicated cutting boards, toasters, and utensils to avoid trace gluten from shared kitchen tools.
Excessive fibre intake before the gut has healed—introduce fibre gradually and choose soluble sources (oat, flax) over insoluble when first eliminating gluten.
Prolonged use of anti-diarrhoeal or antispasmodic medications without addressing the underlying cause; masking symptoms may delay diagnosis of other conditions.
Self-diagnosing and eliminating multiple food groups simultaneously without clinical guidance; this can lead to nutritional deficiency and makes it harder to identify true triggers.
Assuming all digestive symptoms are NCGS-related; other conditions (IBS, FODMAP sensitivity, small-intestinal overgrowth, food allergy, coeliac disease) must be ruled out first.

key_levers.lever: Strict, prolonged gluten elimination (at least 4–6 weeks) with careful verification of true adherence
key_levers.why_it_matters: The only definitive treatment; symptom response (or lack thereof) confirms diagnosis and guides long-term management. Many people declare gluten-free trials a failure due to hidden gluten or cross-contamination; rigorous elimination is essential.
key_levers.how_to_start: Consult a dietitian experienced in coeliac/gluten-free diets to review all food labels, hidden sources, and meal planning. Remove all obvious wheat, barley, rye products and review medications, supplements, and processed foods for gluten.
key_levers.timeline: Symptom improvement typically begins within 3–7 days if NCGS is present; full resolution may take 2–4 weeks. Some people require 6–12 weeks for complete healing.

key_levers.lever: Controlled reintroduction testing (after symptom resolution) to confirm gluten is the true trigger and rule out placebo response
key_levers.why_it_matters: Distinguishes true NCGS from IBS, FODMAP sensitivity, or nocebo effect. Confirms the diagnosis and helps people understand whether lifelong gluten avoidance is necessary or whether occasional intake is tolerable.
key_levers.how_to_start: After 4–6 weeks symptom-free on a gluten-free diet, deliberately add gluten back in a single meal (e.g. 2 slices of bread) and observe for symptom recurrence over the next 1–2 days. Document timing and severity.
key_levers.timeline: Symptom recurrence (if true NCGS) typically occurs within 12–48 hours of reintroduction. If no symptoms occur, NCGS is less likely.

key_levers.lever: Ruling out coeliac disease, wheat allergy, and other mimics via clinical testing before committing to lifelong dietary restriction
key_levers.why_it_matters: Coeliac disease and wheat allergy require medical monitoring and have different long-term implications (risk of complications, family screening). Confirmed NCGS diagnosis avoids unnecessary alarm and focuses management on symptom relief alone.
key_levers.how_to_start: Request blood tests for coeliac serology (tTG-IgA, EMA) and allergy testing (wheat-specific IgE, skin prick test) while still eating gluten. If results are negative and symptoms are gluten-triggered, NCGS becomes the working diagnosis.
key_levers.timeline: Testing can be completed in 1–4 weeks. Results guide next steps and rule out conditions requiring specialist management.

key_levers.lever: Addressing concurrent gut dysfunction (IBS, FODMAP sensitivity, dysbiosis) to avoid incomplete symptom relief
key_levers.why_it_matters: Gluten elimination alone may not resolve symptoms if other triggers (FODMAP, stress, altered microbiome) are present. Comprehensive gut management improves outcomes and prevents frustration.
key_levers.how_to_start: If gluten elimination brings partial improvement only, consider a low-FODMAP trial or discuss small-intestinal overgrowth (SIBO) testing with a gastroenterologist. Assess stress, sleep, and exercise as co-factors.
key_levers.timeline: Low-FODMAP diets show improvement in 2–4 weeks if effective. SIBO testing and treatment take weeks to months.

key_levers.lever: Psychological and stress-management support (CBT, mindfulness, gut-directed hypnotherapy) to address the gut-brain connection
key_levers.why_it_matters: Stress, anxiety, and negative health beliefs amplify symptom perception and delay healing. Functional gut disorders involve bidirectional gut-brain signalling; addressing psychological factors improves outcomes.
key_levers.how_to_start: Discuss with your primary doctor or gastroenterologist; ask for referral to a therapist trained in gut-directed CBT or hypnotherapy. Begin simple stress-reduction practices (daily walks, meditation, yoga) immediately.
key_levers.timeline: CBT typically requires 8–12 sessions over 2–3 months; benefits often seen within weeks. Daily practice of stress reduction shows benefit within days.

key_levers.lever: Nutritional assessment and supplementation to prevent deficiency on a gluten-free diet
key_levers.why_it_matters: Some people on a gluten-free diet consume less fibre, B vitamins, and iron if they rely on highly processed gluten-free products. A balanced, whole-foods gluten-free diet prevents anaemia, constipation, and micronutrient deficiency.
key_levers.how_to_start: Consult a dietitian to review your gluten-free diet for nutritional gaps. Focus on whole grains (rice, quinoa, millet), legumes, vegetables, fruits, and lean proteins. Consider a multivitamin if deficiency is suspected.
key_levers.timeline: Nutritional assessment can be done in a single consultation; changes show benefit in 4–12 weeks (e.g. energy, bowel regularity).

common_mistakes: Eliminating gluten but continuing to consume high-FODMAP foods or heavily processed gluten-free products; symptoms may persist because FODMAP sensitivity or other triggers are unaddressed.

Reintroducing gluten too soon or in small amounts and concluding NCGS is not real; reintroduction testing should be deliberate and include an adequate dose of gluten (e.g. multiple slices of bread, not just one crumb).
Assuming symptom improvement after eliminating gluten proves NCGS; placebo effect is strong in functional disorders. Controlled reintroduction is needed to confirm causality.
Ignoring psychological stress, poor sleep, or anxiety as contributors; these amplify gut sensitivity and can cause symptom persistence despite strict gluten avoidance.
Switching to a diet of highly processed gluten-free products high in sugar, fat, and low in fibre; this worsens metabolic health and may not resolve bloating or digestive dysfunction.

Not checking for cross-contamination or hidden gluten sources; many people believe they are strict but are inadvertently consuming gluten from medications, supplements, soy sauce, or shared kitchen equipment.
Prolonging a restrictive diet without confirming NCGS; if gluten elimination does not improve symptoms significantly after 6–8 weeks, NCGS is less likely and other diagnoses should be explored.
Trying to manage NCGS without professional support; a dietitian and physician together can optimise diagnosis, testing, and long-term management.

specialists.role: Coordinates diagnosis of NCGS by performing upper endoscopy and biopsy to rule out coeliac disease and other organic intestinal pathology. Advises on management and investigates persistent symptoms.
specialists.notes: Often the first specialist involved; expertise in coeliac disease is essential to exclude it. Some gastroenterologists are sceptical of NCGS as a diagnosis because it lacks a definitive biomarker; seek someone open to functional diagnosis.

specialists.type: Registered dietitian (RD) or nutritionist specialising in gastrointestinal or gluten-related disorders
specialists.role: Provides personalised dietary counselling, educates on hidden gluten sources, ensures nutritional adequacy, and helps with elimination-reintroduction protocols. Critical for success.
specialists.notes: Essential partner in NCGS management. A dietitian can prevent malnutrition, identify FODMAP sensitivity, and support sustainable dietary change. Seek one with credentials (RD or equivalent) and experience in coeliac/NCGS.

specialists.role: Performs wheat allergy testing (IgE, skin prick testing) and helps distinguish wheat allergy from NCGS. Advises on emergency management if anaphylaxis risk is present.
specialists.notes: Useful if allergic symptoms are suspected (hives, throat swelling, rapid onset). Less essential if symptoms are clearly gastrointestinal and delayed.

specialists.type: Functional medicine doctor or integrative medicine practitioner
specialists.role: Takes a systems-based approach to NCGS, often investigating gut health, microbiome, SIBO, and other functional factors. May recommend probiotics, enzymes, and supplements.
specialists.notes: Can provide holistic management but may lack formal diagnostic rigour. Ensure they also recommend confirmatory testing (coeliac serology, allergy testing) before diagnosing NCGS.

specialists.type: Therapist or psychologist specialising in gut-directed CBT or hypnotherapy
specialists.role: Addresses the gut-brain connection, helps manage stress and health anxiety, and teaches techniques to reduce symptom amplification. Useful adjunct for persistent functional symptoms.
specialists.notes: Not a replacement for dietary intervention but a valuable complement. Gut-directed hypnotherapy and CBT show evidence in IBS and functional gut disorders.

specialists.type: Primary care physician (GP or internal medicine)
specialists.role: Initial evaluation, referral to specialists, coordination of care, and management of comorbid conditions (depression, anxiety, other chronic disease).
specialists.notes: Essential entry point; a receptive GP who takes functional symptoms seriously is invaluable. Some GPs may be dismissive of NCGS; seek one willing to collaborate with specialists.

patient_communities.name: Coeliac UK and Coeliac Society (various countries)
patient_communities.notes: Primarily focused on coeliac disease but often supports NCGS patients and provides reliable information on gluten-free living, recipes, and product checking.

patient_communities.name: Beyond Celiac (formerly National Foundation for Celiac Awareness)
patient_communities.notes: US-based; provides resources on gluten-related disorders including NCGS, research updates, and community support.

patient_communities.name: Reddit communities (r/glutenfree, r/IBS, r/NCGS)
patient_communities.notes: Peer support and practical advice on gluten-free living, hidden sources, restaurants, and symptom management. Unmoderated; information quality varies; use with caution.

patient_communities.name: FODMAP apps and support groups (Monash University Low FODMAP Diet)
patient_communities.notes: If concurrent FODMAP sensitivity is suspected, Monash's certified app and resources are evidence-based and helpful. Strong community support.

patient_communities.name: IBS Diaries (IBS patient communities)
patient_communities.notes: Useful for IBS-spectrum NCGS patients; provides symptom tracking, peer support, and dietary tips.

patient_communities.name: The Gluten-Free Dietitian (social media and blog)
patient_communities.notes: Evidence-based nutrition education on gluten-free diets and NCGS by a registered dietitian. High-quality, accessible information.

learning_resources.title: The Complete Guide to Gluten and Gluten Sensitivity
learning_resources.description: Comprehensive, evidence-based guide distinguishing coeliac disease, wheat allergy, and NCGS. Covers testing, diagnosis, nutritional concerns, and practical management.

learning_resources.title: Non-Celiac Gluten Sensitivity: A Review
learning_resources.description: Peer-reviewed summary of current understanding of NCGS, mechanisms, diagnostic challenges, and treatment approaches. Technical but thorough.

learning_resources.title: The IBS Solution: Straightforward Advice for Anyone with Irritable Bowel Syndrome
learning_resources.description: Practical guide addressing IBS and overlapping conditions, including gluten sensitivity. Covers dietary and lifestyle strategies.

learning_resources.title: Monash University Low FODMAP Diet: Official App and Resources
learning_resources.description: Evidence-based, world-leading resource for FODMAP elimination and reintroduction. Essential if FODMAP sensitivity is suspected alongside NCGS.

learning_resources.title: Mind-Body Medicine and the Mind-Gut Connection (Podcast and Videos)
learning_resources.author_or_source: Dr. Emeran Mayer (UCLA Digestive Diseases Research Center)
learning_resources.description: Explores the gut-brain connection, stress, and psychosomatic factors in functional gut disorders. Helpful for understanding how stress and anxiety amplify NCGS symptoms.

learning_resources.title: Gut-Directed Hypnotherapy and CBT for IBS
learning_resources.description: Resources on psychological interventions for functional gut disorders, including evidence-based practices applicable to NCGS.

learning_resources.title: The Plant Paradox: The Hidden Dangers in 'Healthy' Foods
learning_resources.description: Discusses lectins and amylase-trypsin inhibitors in wheat as potential triggers of NCGS. Controversial but offers mechanistic theories; use with caution and consult a dietitian.

learning_resources.title: Coeliac UK and Beyond Celiac: Patient-Centred Information Websites
learning_resources.description: Authoritative, regularly updated information on gluten-related disorders, hidden sources of gluten, recipes, and product databases.

learning_resources.title: Gastroenterology Journals and Reviews (Nutrition Reviews, Gastroenterology)
learning_resources.description: Academic literature on NCGS pathophysiology, epidemiology, and management. Useful for those seeking cutting-edge evidence; requires access to journal databases.

learning_resources.title: The Low FODMAP Cookbook: Simple and Tasty Recipes for IBS and Other Digestive Disorders
learning_resources.author_or_source: Heather Eats Treats or similar authors
learning_resources.description: Recipe book combining gluten-free and low-FODMAP principles. Practical for people managing both sensitivities.

sections.relevance_signal: **Symptoms after wheat** without a positive coeliac test. Bloating, gut pain, brain fog, fatigue — improves on gluten-free diet but tTG antibodies are negative.
